author: nero_eth
content:
I see! Yeah, in that case, if we'd do 8237 and clients would stop serving payloads over the CL and completely leave that up for ELs, top-up sync wouldn't work anymore.
<@755590043632140352>, e.g. for prysm, was the plan to just not download payloads (leave that to the EL) but still serve them to clients that want them by requesting them from the EL, or would you've planned to never serve payload requests?
If clients would still do so, we'd get to <@449019668296892420> 's second point "implement 8237, serve payloads" which seems like the best outcome.

The usecase you mention with EL being fully offline is a cool one yeah, I'm just wondering, looking at sync only, wouldn't checkpoint sync on CL + snapv2 on the EL be the most efficient way to catch up?
Then, when the EL is offline for a few blocks, I do see why top-up makes sense compared to using devp2p for requesting headers, bodies, receipts and bals, as it feels cleaner - which would mean we could keep snap sync and adopt top-up after initial sync is done.

author: arnetheduck
content:
> which seems like the best outcome.
it's an outcome that introduces some tech debt (8237 is not .. elegant - it adds yet another hash "kind" and computation work to work around the encoding/hashing difference between EL and CL)

We didn't adopt it on the first reading of the idea for this reason, more or less - in a world where cl's generally can and do download payloads, it's .. redundant (since they can just verify the payloads instead though the various means available) - so everyone would have to add this computation to help clients that don't want to download payloads (but still serve them) - I'd call it a "compromise solution" more than anything.

At that point, I think I'd rather go with option 3: "relax hash verification requirement and make it recommended instead of required" - clients that verify payloads / implement top-up sync will still get the verification benefit and we don't add the debt to the protocol

author: arnetheduck
content:
> I'm just wondering, looking at sync only, wouldn't checkpoint sync on CL + snapv2 on the EL be the most efficient way to catch up?

it depends - checkpoint syncing is a security gap since it's unverified, so you want to avoid it as much as possible ..  in that sense, what we're reasoning about is "how long can my node stay offline and still get be guaranteed to follow the right chain" - it's not so much a performance question but a security question, on the CL side alone - the EL, if it starts snap-syncing mid-way, there are also security implications per our above discussions.

for performance alone, whether snap sync is faster that block-by-block topup depends on many things, ie bandwidth vs disk speed vs computation power vs gas costs vs gas pricing vs the ability to find peers that are willing to give you history (TCP vs QUIC, discovery, peer slots etc) - it's a question with many parameters .. I'd say that for the "typical" upgrade-my-node scenario or "power outage in the middle of the night" wher yueo're cathcing up a few hours, top-up sync is faster .. 

once things stretch into days, resyncing fully might be faster, but since modern execution clients can process the whole ethereum history in a week:ish or two, I'd still go with top-up sync for the peace of mind and simplicity
